github.name: Cognitive-Core-Skills
github.description: A universal, industry-neutral taxonomy of cognitive core skills (perception, memory, reasoning, planning, action, verification, learning, governance) for LLMs, SLMs, AI agents, and world models — with schemas, 159 skill cards, benchmarks, and CI.

github.name: Efficient-LLM-Inference-Serving-Systems
github.description: Why is LLM inference slow — and how do you make it fast? A hands-on, first-principles course: roofline → KV cache → quantization → parallelism → vLLM/SGLang, with GPU labs on open models.

github.name: Awesome-Scientific-LLM-Benchmarks
github.description: A curated, accuracy-first list of benchmarks for evaluating LLMs on scientific reasoning and discovery — math, physics, chemistry, materials, biology, and agentic science.

github.name: qwen-lora-finetune
github.description: Fine-tune Qwen2.5-Coder for code generation on your own dataset using QLoRA. Train on JSONL instruction-response pairs and produce a shareable ~16 MB LoRA adapter.

github.name: Framegen
github.description: Real-time neural frame interpolation in the browser: hand-written WGSL runtime on raw WebGPU, 2.9 MB model, 2x-6x any <video> at 3-4 ms per frame. Chrome extension.

github.name: fortress
github.description: Stealth Chromium engine that stops scrapers and browser agents from getting blocked, with one line of code change.

github.name: OmniPost-AI
github.description: AI-powered Chrome extension for generating and publishing posts to Facebook, Threads, and X using ChatGPT or Gemini.

github.name: Napaxi
github.description: Mobile-native agent SDK for building on-device AI agents with sandboxed execution, extensible tools, and x-app / x-agent / x-channel connectivity.

github.name: lyid-scripts
github.description: Layer Airdrop ID — Premium airdrop automation scripts

github.name: K-12-reverse
github.description: K12-Reverse ChatGPT Creator: Otomatisasi Registrasi Akun ChatGPT Skala Besar dengan Fitur K12 Invite dan Multi-Gmail IMAP.

trends: The current tech landscape is dominated by the rapid, yet challenging, evolution of AI agents, with significant efforts directed at defining their cognitive capabilities and ensuring their secure, ethical deployment. Concurrently, there's a strong emphasis on optimizing LLM performance and cost, alongside their specialization through fine-tuning for niche applications and efficient inference at the edge. AI-powered automation is pervasive, from content generation and social media management to bypassing traditional scraping hurdles. Underpinning these advancements are critical discussions around AI safety, privacy (unlearning), and long-context reasoning. Developers are also increasingly focusing on pragmatic application of AI tools and personal productivity techniques to navigate this fast-paced environment.

ideas.title: Cognitive Agent Skill Evaluator & Benchmark Dashboard
ideas.description: Develop a web-based platform that allows users to define or select 'cognitive skills' (e.g., reasoning, planning, memory) for AI agents, upload custom test cases, and then evaluate various Large Language Models (LLMs) or fine-tuned agents against these benchmarks. The platform would visualize performance across different skills, compare models, and potentially offer insights into areas where an agent underperforms, suggesting targeted fine-tuning strategies. This addresses the growing need for robust, standardized evaluation of agentic AI capabilities beyond simple task completion.
tech_stack: LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, open-source via vLLM/SGLang)
ideas.source_inspiration: Cognitive-Core-Skills, Awesome-Scientific-LLM-Benchmarks, Napaxi, Does code cleanliness affect coding agents?
steps: Research existing cognitive skill taxonomies and LLM evaluation benchmarks (e.g., from 'Cognitive-Core-Skills').
Design a robust database schema to store skills, test datasets, LLM configurations, and evaluation results.
Build a Python backend API to manage skills, test cases, orchestrate LLM calls, and process evaluation metrics.
Integrate with multiple LLM providers (e.g., OpenAI, local models via vLLM/Ollama) to allow comparison.
Develop a responsive frontend dashboard for users to define skills, upload data, run evaluations, and visualize comparative performance metrics.
Implement a reporting feature to generate detailed performance summaries for each evaluated agent/LLM.

ideas.title: Adaptive AI Content & Multi-Platform Publisher
ideas.description: Create a service that intelligently generates tailored content (e.g., social media posts, blog snippets, marketing copy) using an LLM and then automatically publishes it across multiple target platforms. The 'adaptive' aspect involves two key features: (1) dynamic content adjustments based on real-time feedback or A/B testing insights, and (2) integration of 'stealth' automation techniques (inspired by 'fortress') to minimize detection and blocking on platforms that are sensitive to automated interactions. This tool would significantly boost productivity for marketers and content creators.
tech_stack: LLM API (ChatGPT, Gemini, or fine-tuned Qwen2.5-Coder)
Social Media APIs (Facebook Graph API, X API, etc.)
Frontend (Simple web UI with HTML/CSS/JS or Streamlit)
ideas.source_inspiration: OmniPost-AI, fortress, qwen-lora-finetune, Generate parametric, manufacturable 3D models in seconds
steps: Set up a backend service to interface with a chosen LLM for generating diverse content based on user prompts and target platform requirements.
Implement platform-specific publishing modules, utilizing official APIs where available and robust headless browser automation (e.g., Playwright) for others.
Research and integrate 'stealth' techniques into the automation layer (e.g., randomized delays, dynamic user-agent rotation, custom browser fingerprints) inspired by 'fortress'.
Develop a user interface for configuring content types, target platforms, publishing schedules, and monitoring content performance.
Add a basic A/B testing framework or feedback loop to allow content to be iteratively refined based on engagement metrics.
Include a content scheduling and queue management system for automated publishing workflows.

ideas.title: On-Device 'Time-Blindness' Productivity Agent
ideas.description: Build a desktop application that acts as a personalized productivity agent, leveraging a small, local Large Language Model (LLM) or an efficient cloud LLM. This agent would help users combat 'time blindness' by gently monitoring active applications and browser tabs (with explicit user consent), understanding user-defined 'lock-in' tasks, and providing non-intrusive, context-aware interventions. Examples include smart notifications about task drift, summaries of current progress, or suggestions for breaks. The agent could also analyze usage patterns over time to suggest optimal work/break durations, directly addressing modern productivity challenges.
tech_stack: Python (for backend logic and local LLM interaction via API)
Ollama / Hugging Face Transformers (for local LLM inference)
ideas.source_inspiration: Napaxi, You're Not Lazy — You're Time Blind. Here's How Lock In Fixes It., A New Personal Best: What Six Months of Locking In Can Do
steps: Develop the core Electron application structure capable of monitoring active windows/applications and browser tab titles (with user permissions).
Integrate a local LLM (e.g., via `ollama` or a small model from Hugging Face Transformers) to interpret user-defined tasks and current activity contextually.
Implement a 'lock-in' timer and a dynamic notification system that delivers context-aware prompts or progress summaries based on LLM insights.
Design a local database (e.g., SQLite) to store usage patterns, task durations, and user-defined productivity goals.
Build a dashboard within the application to visualize 'time spent' vs. 'intended time' and highlight patterns associated with time blindness.
Add an 'AI suggestions' feature that proposes optimal work/break intervals or workflow adjustments based on historical personal productivity data.

quick_actions: Action 1: Clone `qwen-lora-finetune` and fine-tune a small open-source LLM (like Qwen2.5-Coder) on a small, custom dataset (e.g., 50-100 JSONL instruction-response pairs) to understand the practical aspects of LLM specialization.

Action 2: Schedule and commit to at least one 60-90 minute 'Lock In' deep work session today or tomorrow, actively eliminating distractions and focusing on a single high-priority task, as highlighted in the Dev.to productivity articles.

Action 3: Install `ollama` or explore a small, on-device AI agent SDK like `Napaxi`'s concepts. Brainstorm 2-3 personal automation tasks (e.g., local file summarization, intelligent file organization) that such an agent could perform locally.

Action 4: Spend 30 minutes reviewing the core concepts from `Efficient-LLM-Inference-Serving-Systems`, focusing on techniques like KV cache, quantization, and parallelism. Understanding these will be crucial for any future LLM deployment.
